COMPANY BRIEFS
Cities of Despair
New Camcorder Features Allow the User to Play Director
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHILE the cost of gas goes through the roof, take solace: the camcorders used for saving vacation memories now have more power and more features, including even basic models. And many will bring the beach and mountains back home to the  living room for a discount price of around $500, instead of the $1,000 of a few  years ago. 

     As prices have dropped, camcorders have grown in popularity. According to the Electronics Industries Association, a trade group, nearly one in four families owns a camcorder and more than 3.5 million will be sold this year. Many of them  will record graduations, vacations, weddings and the baby's first steps, but they'll do it better, with built-in color screens and stereo sound and picture quality that surpasses a laser disk. Some will even allow you to edit tapes and  add special effects. (You pay more for the extras.)

Currents;Children's Book Artists as Muralists
nytimes_articles.abstract: FOUR children's book illustrators are creative partners in one of New York's  most colorful day-care centers: a 70-foot mural adorns the new headquarters of Women in Need, at 115 West 31st Street, designed by Jeffrey Murphy. The group provides housing for homeless women and their children. Rita Zimmer, its founder, said, "We have women who want to go back to work and who want to come in to the alcohol- and substance-abuse center for treatment but don't want to put their children in foster care. This center is for them." The mural, by Lisa  Desimini, Dan Yaccarino, Sara Schwartz and David Ricceri, is destined for a children's book.

City at the Crossroads
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The key to peace in Bosnia is a small river city called Brcko. 

     This flat little city lies at the narrowest point of the 15-mile-long Serb-held corridor along the Sava River, the border between Bosnia and Croatia.  For the Serbs, the corridor is the link between Serbia and the Serb-held territories in northwestern Bosnia. For the Bosnian Government in Sarajevo, it is a wall that denies Bosnians access to Croatia and the rest of Europe.

Real Estate;In Edgewater, N.J., luxury apartment construction is reviving on the Hudson shoreline.
nytimes_articles.abstract:
By August, work will begin on transforming a patch of the Hudson River shoreline here into a luxury apartment enclave, ending an eight-year dry spell in new residential construction. 

     The $40 million project, the River Club, will also be the first rental to rise amid the condominium and cooperative developments that dot the 3.5-mile-long old industrial waterfront of this Bergen County borough at the base of the Palisade cliffs, south of the George Washington Bridge. Plans call for 266 one- and two-bedroom apartments in seven brick-and-stucco buildings of three stories. The buildings will be clustered into two groups, framing a courtyard and open space.

Caught in Milosevic's Web
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the United States and its partners pulled together the Bosnian peace accord, they tried to ensnare the region's warmongers in a web of peace. Six months later, it's hard to tell who ensnared whom. 

     The Dayton accord is a tangled web. The President of Serbia, Slobodan Milosevic, who incited and sustained ethnic cleansing and the partition of Bosnia, made the accord possible, but he has not fulfilled his important promises. Last Sunday, he reportedly told Secretary of State Warren Christopher  that he cannot remove the Bosnian Serb leader Radovan Karadzic and Gen. Ratko Mladic, indicted war criminals who foil progress at every turn.

Clean Needles Saved My Life
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Ten years ago, while I was waiting around for my heroin connection, a friend  saved my life. I was at risk for AIDS, she said. To protect myself, I should always use my own works or, if I had to share, I should first clean the needle thoroughly with bleach and water. 

     Thanks to her, I was not infected when I quit two years later. I had survived two diseases: depression, which led me to cocaine and heroin while at Columbia University, and addiction. I was just lucky to have escaped a fatal third.
